title: Implementing Hierarchical Task Decomposition for LLM Agents
summary: A structured procedure for decomposing a complex goal into an executable task graph — recursively splitting non-atomic tasks into ordered subtasks with typed dependency edges, depth limits, cycle detection, and parallelism annotation. Derived from KB-AG-1: decomposition is the first operation the Planner agent runs before dispatching tasks to Executors via handoff contracts.
preconditions: A goal or task description is available as input.
The goal cannot be completed in a single tool call or LLM prompt — it requires sequencing.

cost_estimate.notes: LLM-guided decomposition (step 3) is automatable with a structured output prompt. Cycle detection (step 5) is algorithmic. Atomicity boundary judgment (step 2) and max-depth flagging require configuration and validation logic.

steps.action: Parse the goal into a root node with a unique id, description, status=pending, and empty children array.
steps.rationale: The root node is the contract between the caller and the Planner. It must be parseable by the Executor without ambiguity. Every subsequent step extends this graph — starting with a well-formed root prevents malformed graph state from propagating downstream.

tooling.automated: Root node schema: { id: 'root', description: string, status: 'pending', children: [], is_atomic: false, depth: 0 }. Validate: description is non-empty, id is unique within graph.
tooling.manual: Rewrite the goal as a single, unambiguous sentence with a clear done condition. If you cannot write a done condition, the goal is underspecified — clarify before decomposing.

steps.action: Define the atomicity boundary: a task is atomic when a single tool call, a single LLM prompt, or a single deterministic function can complete it with no internal branching.
steps.rationale: Without a clear atomicity boundary, decomposition either stops too early (leaving non-executable leaf nodes) or recurses indefinitely (producing over-decomposed micro-tasks the Executor cannot parallelise efficiently). The atomicity definition is the shared contract between Planner and Executor — document it explicitly.

tooling.automated: Atomicity test prompt: 'Can this task be completed by calling a single function, tool, or prompt with no if/else branching? Task: {task_description}. Answer: yes | no. If no, explain what branching is required.'
tooling.manual: Decision heuristic — atomic if: (a) maps directly to one tool call schema, OR (b) fits in one LLM prompt with a single output type, OR (c) is a single deterministic computation (sort, filter, format). Non-atomic if: requires a decision, has multiple output types, or contains sub-goals.

steps.action: Recursively decompose each non-atomic node: prompt the LLM with the node description and atomicity definition; receive a structured JSON list of child tasks with dependency edges.
steps.rationale: Unstructured decomposition produces inconsistent task granularity and missing dependencies. A structured output prompt with schema validation forces the LLM to make dependency edges explicit — preventing silent coupling between tasks that should be independent.

tooling.automated: Decomposition prompt: 'Decompose this task into subtasks. Task: {node.description}. Atomicity rule: {atomicity_definition}. Output JSON only: { subtasks: [{ id: string, description: string, is_atomic: boolean, depends_on: string[] }] }. Rules: (1) each subtask is smaller in scope than the parent, (2) depends_on lists only sibling ids, (3) if depends_on is empty the task can start immediately.' Validate JSON schema before accepting. Re-prompt once on schema failure; abort on second failure.
tooling.manual: After LLM decomposition, read each subtask description aloud. Ask: does this task have exactly one done condition? Is it smaller in scope than its parent? If either answer is no, re-decompose that node.

steps.action: Enforce maximum decomposition depth. Default: 4 levels. At the depth limit, mark remaining non-atomic tasks as leaf nodes and add them to flagged_nodes for human review.
steps.rationale: Without a depth limit, decomposition recurses indefinitely on underspecified or circular goals. Flagged nodes are not failures — they are signals that the goal at that node needs clarification before execution. Returning them as warnings allows the pipeline to make partial progress while surfacing the ambiguity.

tooling.automated: Depth check: traverse task_graph_draft BFS-order. For each node at depth >= max_depth: set node.is_leaf = true, add to flagged_nodes with { node_id, description, depth, reason: 'max_depth_reached' }. Do not recurse further.
tooling.manual: Review flagged_nodes after decomposition. For each: ask whether the task is genuinely non-atomic or whether it is under-specified. If under-specified, clarify the goal and re-run decomposition on that branch.

steps.action: Run cycle detection on the dependency graph using Kahn's algorithm. Return the cycle path as an error and abort if a cycle is found.
steps.rationale: A cycle in the dependency graph means two tasks each require the other to complete first — an unresolvable deadlock. Cycles most often appear when the LLM hallucinated a dependency edge or when two tasks are genuinely circular (indicating a design flaw in the goal decomposition). Abort, not just warn — a cyclic graph cannot be executed.

tooling.automated: Kahn's algorithm: (1) build in_degree map for all nodes, (2) initialise queue with all nodes where in_degree == 0, (3) while queue non-empty: dequeue node, decrement in_degree of all children, enqueue children where in_degree reaches 0, (4) if processed_count < total_nodes: cycle exists. Return cycle path: run DFS from any unprocessed node to find the cycle members.
tooling.manual: If cycle detected: inspect the depends_on arrays of the cycle members. Identify the edge that represents the LLM hallucination or the genuine circular dependency. Either remove the erroneous edge or re-decompose the cyclic nodes into a linear sequence.

steps.action: Annotate parallel opportunities: tasks at the same depth level with no dependency edges between them can execute concurrently. Set execution_mode=parallel on these nodes.
steps.rationale: Sequential execution of parallelisable tasks multiplies latency unnecessarily. Parallelism annotation at planning time — not at execution time — allows the Executor to dispatch concurrently without needing to re-analyse the graph at runtime. Only annotate parallel when the Executor infrastructure explicitly supports concurrent dispatch.

tooling.automated: For each depth level: group nodes with no edges between them → mark execution_mode=parallel. Group nodes with edges → mark execution_mode=sequential. Output: { node_id, execution_mode, parallel_group_id? }.
tooling.manual: Sanity check: for each parallel group, verify that running the tasks in any interleaved order produces the same result. If order matters, there is a missing dependency edge — add it.

steps.action: Produce the final execution plan: topologically sorted task list with agent role assignments, dependency edges, and parallelism annotations.
steps.rationale: The execution plan is the output contract from the Planner to the Executor layer (KB-AG-1 handoff contract: Planner → Executor). It must be a valid topological ordering of the validated task graph — each task appears after all its dependencies. This plan also feeds directly into the evaluation pipeline (KB-AG-3): regression tests run against the execution plan structure.

tooling.automated: Topological sort output schema: { plan_id: uuid, goal: string, tasks: [{ id, description, agent_role, depends_on: [], execution_mode: 'sequential' | 'parallel', parallel_group_id: string | null, is_atomic: true, depth: number }], flagged_nodes: [], max_depth_used: number, total_tasks: number }
tooling.manual: Validate the plan by simulation: mentally execute the first three tasks in order. Do their outputs satisfy the inputs of their dependents? If not, a dependency edge is missing. Fix in the validated_task_graph and re-sort.

successConditions: All leaf nodes satisfy the atomicity definition
Cycle detection passes — topological sort completes on all nodes
Decomposition depth does not exceed the configured max_depth
All task ids are unique within the graph
execution_plan is a valid topological ordering of validated_task_graph
Each parallel group contains only tasks with no dependency edges between members
failureConditions: Cycle detected in dependency graph — abort execution, return cycle path
LLM decomposition produces invalid JSON on two consecutive attempts — abort and surface error to caller
A leaf node at max_depth remains non-atomic — add to flagged_nodes, do not abort
Two tasks share the same id — id collision corrupts dependency resolution, abort

inputs.name: goal
inputs.description: The complex goal to decompose into an executable task graph

inputs.name: atomicity_rule
inputs.description: Natural language definition of what makes a task atomic in this context

outputs.name: execution_plan
outputs.description: Topologically sorted task list with role assignments, dependencies, and parallelism annotations — output contract to Executor layer (KB-AG-1)

outputs.name: flagged_nodes
outputs.description: Tasks at max_depth that remain non-atomic — require human review or goal clarification
